title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community theatre. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Grace, The Backstage Organiser
Grace joined the theatre because she wanted a quiet job away from the stage, but she soon became responsible for props. [47]Her first task was building a complete list of objects that had to appear in each scene, including which side of the stage they should enter from.[/47] [48]She had worked as a wedding planner, and that background helped her remain calm when actors asked for missing hats five minutes before the curtain went up.[/48] At first she underestimated how heavy the scenery would be, and she went home with bruised shoulders after every rehearsal. What she enjoyed most was seeing shy volunteers become confident once they trusted the backstage system. Grace now says the audience never notices good organisation, which is exactly the point. She still keeps a tiny notebook in her pocket, because she learned that one pencil, plus a spare roll of tape, can rescue a whole performance.
texts.B:
### Nico, The Lighting Assistant
Nico was studying engineering when a neighbour invited him to see how the theatre used its old lighting desk. [49]He was fascinated to discover that most of the equipment still worked through numbered sliders rather than a modern screen.[/49] The technical side suited him, but he was surprised by how much artistic judgement was involved in deciding whether a face looked warm, frightened or tired. [50]The hardest moment came during the opening night, when a fuse failed and he had to reroute two lamps while the actors continued speaking.[/50] After that, he began preparing spare cables before every show, even for short amateur productions. [56]Nico says volunteering made him listen more carefully to directors, because a tiny change in brightness can alter the meaning of a scene.[/56] He has also become less impatient with old machines, since they often reveal their logic if you watch them closely and write down their habits after rehearsal.
texts.C:
### Priya, The Costume Repairer
Priya had sewn only for herself before she answered a notice asking for help with costumes. [51]She quickly learned that repairing clothes for actors meant strengthening seams so they could survive repeated quick changes in the dark.[/51] [55]Her favourite discovery was that a slightly uneven stitch was invisible from the audience, which made her less perfectionist.[/55] The biggest challenge was sharing a tiny room with rails of coats, boxes of shoes, loose feathers and three people holding needles. [52]She was especially proud when a teenager playing the lead said the repaired jacket finally made him feel like the character.[/52] Priya found the theatre more welcoming than she had expected, especially on evenings when the cast applauded the wardrobe team, partly because everyone depended on someone else's invisible work. She now teaches new volunteers how to label garments properly, as a lost scarf can delay an entire scene even if nobody outside the wardrobe room knows its name.
texts.D:
### Martin, The Front-of-House Host
Martin started helping at the ticket desk after his daughter joined the youth cast. [53]He had assumed the role would involve little more than smiling and handing out programmes, but he soon had to learn how to calm latecomers without interrupting the first scene.[/53] His previous work in a hotel proved useful when visitors complained about restricted views or lost reservations. What affected him most was meeting older residents who said the theatre was the only place where they regularly spoke to neighbours. [54]He began keeping a note of patrons who needed aisle seats, so they would not have to explain their mobility problems every time.[/54] Martin says front-of-house work taught him that hospitality is not decoration but part of the performance. He still feels nervous before a full house, especially on wet evenings when everyone arrives together, although the nerves now make him more alert and kinder to strangers.

questions.question: mentions creating a detailed record of items needed during the performance?
questions.explanation: Grace made a complete list of props and where they should enter from.

questions.question: says their previous event-planning experience helped them stay composed under pressure?
questions.explanation: Grace’s wedding-planning background helped her remain calm when actors needed missing items.

questions.question: was intrigued by the unexpectedly old-fashioned way the equipment was operated?
questions.explanation: Nico discovered that the lighting desk still worked through numbered sliders.

questions.question: describes solving an electrical problem while the show was already in progress?
questions.explanation: Nico rerouted lamps during opening night after a fuse failed.

questions.question: learned that clothing used on stage needed extra durability for rapid changes?
questions.explanation: Priya strengthened seams so costumes could survive repeated quick changes.

questions.question: felt proud because a repair helped an actor connect with a role?
questions.explanation: Priya was proud when the lead actor said the jacket made him feel like the character.

questions.question: realised that greeting the audience required more tact than they had expected?
questions.explanation: Martin had to calm latecomers without interrupting the first scene.

questions.question: kept personal seating information to make future visits easier for some audience members?
questions.explanation: Martin noted patrons who needed aisle seats so they would not need to explain again.

questions.question: mentions discovering that minor imperfections could not be seen by the audience?
questions.explanation: Priya learned that a slightly uneven stitch was invisible from the audience.

questions.question: says volunteering changed the way they listened to creative instructions?
questions.explanation: Nico says he learned to listen more carefully to directors.
